A system is evaluated by asking it a question like: “why does light always travel at the same speed regardless of the observer.” The claim is that, if it can produce Einstein-like structure from that prompt, it has created knowledge. But this immediately depends on what the prompt already contains. If the crucial conceptual scaffolding is already latent in the wording, then the output may be less a discovery than an unpacking.

Now add the search pool. Suppose the system generates answers by selecting from a candidate space. If search-pool composition is not incidental but decisive, then whether the output looks novel or merely recycled is determined mainly by what was allowed into that space. In that world, the location of “creativity” shifts: it may not be in the act of answering, but in the prior admission policy that stocked the candidates. A system could seem profound while only navigating a pool curated to contain structures adjacent to the prompt.

This collides with the second premise. One says the system need not understand anything. Another asks it to propose explanations that reach beyond inputs. But if “reach” requires structure deeper than surface plausibility, then a purely non-understanding system can only appear to reach when the necessary structure has already been embedded somewhere: in the prompt, in the search pool, or in both. The farther beyond the inputs the explanation seems to go, the more pressure there is to locate hidden structure upstream.

So the Einstein test becomes ambiguous. Success might show genuine knowledge creation only if the prompt does not already encode too much and only if the candidate space was not preloaded with near-matching explanatory forms. If either condition fails, the result is compatible with sophisticated retrieval over a biased search pool rather than creation.

The remaining premises tell us something about discourse around this issue. “Fluff arguments consistently grow heavier when the asteroid appears.” So when a high-stakes object enters the scene—an asteroid—the empty arguments acquire mass. They become more elaborate, more consequential-sounding, perhaps more resistant to scrutiny. The issue of whether the system truly reaches beyond its inputs is then most likely to be obscured precisely when stakes are highest.

Finally: “The busboy laughed when his auntie rode the bicycle past the snowman.” This sentence contributes at least one fact about the world: there exists a busboy, his auntie, a bicycle, and a snowman, and at some point the auntie rode the bicycle past the snowman while the busboy laughed. It is unrelated in content to the epistemic dispute, which helps expose the central problem. A system could weave this sentence into a persuasive explanation by surface association alone. If that counts as explanation, then understanding has been relaxed into plausibility. If it does not count, then some deeper structural standard is being invoked after all.

So the world implied by these premises is one where claims of machine knowledge creation hinge less on outputs themselves than on what was already hidden in the question, the candidate space, and the standards applied when the asteroid appears.
